prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response:
“Gracias a la Vida” by Violeta Parra.  
She wrote it only months before taking her own life, yet every line is a quiet, stubborn thank-you to existence itself—thanking the very air that will later forget her. The melody is almost a lullaby, but the words keep opening wider, like hands that refuse to close into fists. When she sings “gracias a la vida que me ha dado tanto,” the gratitude feels wrestled, not sentimental, as if she’s squeezing light out of a wound. I cry because the song doesn’t ask for comfort; it just shows how fiercely we can love the world even while it breaks our heart.
